speaker: interviewer
text: February 28, 1996. An interview with Alex Stern. This is tape four. Can you tell me again what was the name of the camp that you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Flossenburg. Flossenburg. When we left Flossenburg, I mentioned Regensburg, that's another city past Flossenburg. We left Flossenburg with SS all around, behind us and everywhere else. And they made us, again, rush and walk and rush and walk. And suddenly they stopped us. And they told us to go down, in a-- in a-- in a-- from the highway down [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] between the trees. We should run to save our lives. And when we we were all down from the highway, they started shooting machine guns. And that's the last thing I remember. I got shot somehow and fell down. And next time I woke up, I was in hospital. I must have been unconscious, God knows how long. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many prisoners were with you when you left Flossenburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were a lot of us. They emptied the whole camp. I was between, in the middle of the group walking from there. But there were-- in Flossenburg, there were about 16,000 people. So when we started walking, our group [INAUDIBLE], you know, kept on walking and walking people one after another. And it was, again, a big rush, rush, run, run, go, go. And the shooting is the last thing I remember. And then I woke up in the German hospital. And-- when I saw I'm alive, I said, I must be in heaven, because after what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then a- a-- a German nurse came over and spoke to me in German. I understood everything, I spoke German pretty well in those years. And the only thing I realized, there was a cross over my head. So I asked her, what's that? She said, you're at a German Catholic hospital, whatever it was. So I was laying there. I saw myself with the intravenous. So I felt very weak. I couldn't even move my head.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was laying there like, God knows how long. And then an American doctor came in and checked on me and looked at me and start talking to me in Yiddish. So I ask him if I'm in [NON-ENGLISH], in Heaven. He said, no, no, no, you're here in the hospital in Germany. Well, to me he was an angel, no matter how you slice it. And he was my savior. He says, you know, we picked you up on the side of the highway. And you were unconscious. And he told me the whole story. I said, oh, my God. He kept coming to me for weeks. And every time he came in, he brought me something. And he was talking to me in Yiddish. I was very nervous. I was very agitated. I was afraid. I was still-- didn't know what's going on. And he explained to me, look, you're in a hospital. You're in safe hands. It's American army here. We take care of you. And he came and used to stay there with me for about ten minutes, a lot longer than he was talking to another. [INAUDIBLE] a lot of sick people there. And after that-- he was talking to me. I was trying to get up the first time. I had no strength, even to move my head. It took a long while until I was able to get to myself. And when I started walking, he kept on coming. He came and he explained to me that you'd better watch yourself. You're still on the critical list, like they would say today. You're not allowed to eat yet, but you want to. They used to give me food, whatever he told them to. And it-- it-- it saved my life. Because after I left the hospital, they explain me what happened in that area.

speaker: interviewer
text: So a lot of people started to eat whatever they found. And their stomach wasn't able to digest it or-- or bear with it. And they just plain died. And do you remember the name of the doctor who helped you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wish I would. I wish I would remember his name. To me, I still call him an angel, a [NON-ENGLISH]. I said he was a [NON-ENGLISH] came down from Heaven and took care of me. All I know is that he told me he was from Philadelphia. That I couldn't forget. I never knew what Philadelphia is, or even if it exists. He said he was a butcher's son. He was a doctor. And that's all I-- and we were together for a long while. And then suddenly they decided that they-- they are taking the-- the Russian. Or, not prisoners-- the-- the Russians who were in the prison camp, or they were soldiers before. And they took the Russians out of there and the hospital was half empty. Oh, by the way, they took whatever the [INAUDIBLE] souvenir I had. I had my stripe from concentration camp. They thought it was a fortune. I had a little box. They took it away. I've never found it after that, but that's the way it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever find out where Philadelphia was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Did I ever? I went to Philadelphia. I was trying to find any butcher there. And I didn't know the name. So I went with my brother, may he rest in peace. He was-- he was an American for a longer time. And not only I couldn't find the doctor. You know, I mean, I thought Philadelphia would be easy. We went once to a Jewish community kind there. And I told them what happened to me, and they-- they couldn't help me. I didn't go again, but I will always be thankful to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever find out how many people survived from the shooting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's something I really want to talk to you about. And first, I want to tell you that when I was out on my foot, I got an apartment in Cham that-- there, where the hospital was. And this doctor told me, don't you try to go home east, because everything is communism there. So I was looking for somebody who goes back to my town. I found a girl. And I gave her my name and address where I am. And if you ever get home, whoever you find from my family, tell them I'm not moving from here until I find out that somebody is alive. Let them know. And a month didn't go by that my brother came. One morning, he knocks on my door. And it was the happiest day of my life. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: The oldest, Itzak. He wasn't in the concentration camp. He was-- he was liberated earlier. He was liberated in '44. And by the way, I was liberated April 23rd. And he came, and he told me that my sister's alive. And she didn't want to come with him. She didn't want to leave. We had a pretty well-established place there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which sister is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ella. That she does not want to leave our hometown, and she wants to stay there. And I said to my brother, look, you stay here. You watch my apartment. They gave us a very good, decent apartment in those years, the first liberated there in Cham. And I go back, and I-- and I bring her to Germany. So I went home. She's still was a little bit-- hard to convince her that this is no future for Jewish people. And I couldn't even-- the few days I was there, I couldn't stand it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there were people came back. One of my uncles had a wedding after I came home. And there were a lot of uncles and cousins. Ours were a very big family there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And finally, I convinced my sister to come out-- go out. And I came out with her. And we stopped in Budapest. My aunt was there, and there when I met my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife's name is Julia. And we met at my aunt's house in Budapest. And we became very attached to each other. She only had one sister, and she was married. And she said she doesn't think she has much to wait for in that miserable place, what we called after the war. And I gave her my address. And I said, whenever you have a chance-- because there were transports coming out from Budapest, I think it was every week-- all you have to do is register. It went through the Haganah, and other Jewish groups that they were taking Jews out of Eastern Europe. And I went very soon. I was in Budapest a short time. And my transport went out. We stopped in Vienna, I came with my sister, of course. And we went back to Germany. And a few weeks later, my wife came. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: At this time, when you were traveling, how did your wounds heal? Did you have any problems?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was quite a while later. I-- I had typhus after. I was still in the hospital for a while. And then when I came back the second time, I had problem with my thyroids. They were infected. So nothing went so easy. But at that age, you survive, once you survive concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: The doctor who took care of you. Did he tell you where you were shot? Where your wounds were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, I was shot in my back, middle of my back. He said he couldn't believe that I was alive when he saw that hole in the middle of my back. And I was shot on my shoulders, that was a pretty open wound at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was a miracle, that's what he said. If he were doctor, he said to me, can you move your-- your legs? Your back-- there was nothing paralyzed. He couldn't believe it. He said, God was with you. I said, he certainly was. Did they ever tell you how they found you still alive, when so many had been killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. First of all, the doctor told me. And then after 50 years, we went to a hotel near New York, in Long Island, an orthodox hotel. And they had a pamphlet about the Jewish-- the Young Israel-- every season sends out a pamphlet like that. And my wife was reading it. And she says, hey, look, Cham is in this pamphlet. So I started to read. And there was a group from Passaic that went to visit Flossenburg. And I found out things that over 50 years-- I-- I was reading books and films and everything else. And I never knew exactly what happened in that march when people left Flossenburg. I know that I survived, but I didn't know exactly what happened. But I got a little more picture of that one. Because I was really unconscious. But when I found out, I said, this is just unbelievable. After 50 years, you come across something like this. And I intended to get in touch with the rabbi from Passaic. When I go home, I will-- I will give it another shot. My wife had an operation, pretty heavy, and I couldn't get in touch with him before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find out how many inmates survived the shootings?

speaker: interviewee
text: According to this pamphlet, there were five people left alive. So they have some names in it. But there were so many happenings in those years. And if I had a chance to get together and talk with these people about what happened in those years there, I probably will get more information. But it's-- it's amazing that after 50 years-- and this is something to prove, that people try to deny that the Holocaust never happened. That things that happened 50 years ago to me, that I am a Holocaust survivor-- after 50 years, I read about something that I couldn't-- I couldn't find out 50 years ago, it's amazing. And there are those people today that try to say that it never happened. I mean, it's just unbelievable. And this is one thing that I-- I want to appreciate, the work you guys are doing and-- and Spielberg. God bless him. I-- I can't talk enough about this. This is one of the other miracles, that I survived and-- and Spielberg came along to get my story on-- on a film. It's just unbelievable. I'm telling you, he should really be well. And-- and God should bless him, and all of you. You're wonderful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. What about your marriage? When did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was in Cham. That's another story. And so my wife came after us. We got-- we had a big community after the war, in Cham. That's another thing. I want to talk with those people that went to Flossenburg in Cham. My wife got married. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They make a wedding gown that I have it in a picture. Three girls-- two sisters and my wife-- got married with the same gown. And they all live around here now. We get in touch, we talk to each other-- the same gown. This gown, a Hungarian lady made it. She was a seamstress. It was beautiful. Those years, oh, you wouldn't believe it either. We made it from a parachute that was white material.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what country the parachute came from?

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been Germany, I guess. We-- we bought it on the black market, so it's something that-- it's-- it's history.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a Jewish wedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely, we had a wedding. We had a [NON-ENGLISH]. We had everything. We had an orthodox rabbi in Cham. We had everything there. It was very, very religious. We had a committee, and Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur. We had services. We-- look, that was after the war. People came together from all over the world.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we never moved from Cham until we left to Cuba. And that's another thing I wanted to mention to you. How did we go to Cuba?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you before that my mother used to send a letter to my uncle every week. And I used to take it to the post office. And I remembered my uncle's address. So we figured when we were in Germany, I'm going to write a letter to my uncle that we are here. I had an address in Germany. And they should know that, you know, his sister's children, we are here. And in few weeks, we got a letter. Don't you move from whatever you are, I'm going to send you all the papers you need and the tickets. And you're going to come to Cuba. Well, otherwise, we would have gone to Israel. But it was so hard to get in those years. I mean, they always give us a hard time with those Cyprus stories and Exodus. And they wouldn't let us go nowhere. So I figured if I have a chance to go to an uncle in Cuba and he was-- two uncles. And they were desperate for us to go to Cuba, so we went there. So we had a pretty good life there until Castro got the whole island in the Communist situation.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you arrive in Cuba?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1947. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I went there with my brother and sister and my wife, of course. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We settled down there, but my brother and sister decided that they were going to go to the United States. So we stayed in Cuba for another 13 years. My brother and sister came to the United States. And they lived here. We used to visit them, my wife used to go to my sister's house. And we stayed in Cuba. We were very satisfied there. And if it wouldn't have been for Castro, we probably would have stayed there. But I left there, and I-- I learned a trade. And I made pretty good use of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a business did you have in Cuba?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Cuba, we had-- we were manufacturing dresses, sportswear. And it was a pretty good going business. We-- we did well. We could afford whatever was necessary.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, we had three maids in the house. And when we left Cuba, my only worry was that my wife was used to have three maids. How is she going to do without them? So I have to tell you, God bless her. First thing she came to United States, she was trying to get to work and start up a life from new. And kids were taken care of. And I was taken care of. And we worked, and we had a pretty good life. Thank God for that. Tell us about your kids.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my son Abraham, he was born in Cuba. He just got married a few months ago. And he has a big daughter. She lives in Seattle. And now he's got two more with my daughter-in-law. And my sister Margaret, she also was born in Cuba. She was five years when we got here. And she's married. She's got two boys, wonderful kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me the name of the grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, of course. My grandson David, he just got bar mitzvah last year, another of the happiest days of my life. Next step is he's going to get married. And that's what I'm looking forward for. I'm hoping we will live. Of course, he made high school. And he's going to make college, very smart boy. And the little guy, Joshua, is just in his brother's step. All life, God bless the both of them. I owe them a lot, they make my life comfortable. Something to look forward and enjoyable. They really are, God bless the both of them. Very happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have the two boys, how about the other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two boys, two boys. And, of course, Jackie, the other children, Melissa and Stacy. They-- they love me just as much as they do-- than Abe-- than David and Joshua. And I love the kids, they're wonderful, well-behaved children. And we going looking forward to have a little [NON-ENGLISH] for the grandchildren. [INAUDIBLE] it comes to show you that Hitler did not accomplish his-- his point. I mean, we're still people who believe in Jewish life. And we believe in the future of the Jewish communities. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and whenever you talk about whatever they were trying to accomplish to do to the Jews, they did not even though the biggest part of us disappeared. But that's-- that's their murder case, not ours. We did our best to survive what we could. And whoever had years survived, and who didn't perished. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think helped you to survive in the camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Number one, I was always well-built. I was strong boy. I used to pick up both of my brothers, both of them. They were both older than I was. I used to pick them up, each in one hand. I was the strongest of all the three of them. And I guess besides having years, I survived. Because I was working. I never fell back. I always tried to survive. When we were walking, even in those dead marches, I walked. I walked. I was pushed, I walked. Without being pushed, I walked. I always tried to. The only time they shoot me, I was unconscious, and I still survived. So it's half medical, and half being able to survive. That's what it is. And I hope it never, never, never gives them a chance to-- to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] show the bad part that was during Fascism, or Nazis, or whatever they called it. To me, it was open murder. It wasn't the German people that supposed to be civilized and-- and know bad from wrong. Just plain murderers, they were just plain murderers. They had no mother, they have nobody to have respect for, if they could do things that they did. That's all I can tell about it. And I do hope that our [NON-ENGLISH] survive. And we're going to be able to go there. I used to go every year. I went for a month to Israel for 12 years. I went every year. We was there a month every year. And we enjoyed it. I have a very big family in Israel and a proud family. I hope that the-- the country will be able to-- to survive between all those Arabs. Hitler couldn't get away with us, I-- I am sure they're not going to be able to get rid of us. So pray to God that this exists.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one more thing, when we went to-- I went with my children to the Holocaust in-- in Washington. At that time, that was even before Spielberg. And I said to my kids, you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is one of my happiest days in my life. Because if anything, when I was a young [NON-ENGLISH] before, but to see a Holocaust memorial in Washington that is not a Jewish country. And they were able to collect money and to do such a wonderful work that they did. They are never going to be able to say that the Holocaust never existed, it's a made up thing. It existed and killed the best of us. [CRYING] [SOBBING] And we survivors are going to fight to have the Jewish people live on forever. [NON-ENGLISH]. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's-- that's the message I want to leave to my children, to remember that they are Jewish and to be proud of their heritage as long as they live. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I hope they pay attention to whatever happened, not to let it happen again. God bless you all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Why do you think that Israel is important to the Jewish people in other parts of the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, let me put it to you my way. Israel exists today because 6 million of us perished. If not, we would have never had a country. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The British would have never let Jewish people in Israel-- in Palestine, what they called that before. They still try to sell out Israel for oil or for whatever it is. But I do hope that as long as there is a-- a-- a future for Israel, the Jewish religion and the Jewish being will survive.
